full_notes:
Thermodynamics is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that unit 6 EqUilibriUm Chemical equilibria are important in numerous biological and environmental processes.

The text explains that for example, equilibria involving O2 molecules and the protein hemoglobin play a crucial role in the transport and delivery of O 2 from our lungs to our muscles.

The text explains that similar equilibria involving CO molecules and hemoglobin account for the toxicity of CO.

The text explains that when a liquid evaporates in a closed container, molecules with relatively higher kinetic energy escape the liquid surface into the vapour phase and number of liquid molecules from the vapour phase strike the liquid surface and are retained in the liquid phase.

The text explains that it gives rise to a constant vapour pressure because of an equilibrium in which the number of molecules leaving the liquid equals the number returning to liquid from the vapour.

The text explains that we say that the system has reached equilibrium state at this stage.

The text explains that however, this is not static equilibrium and there is a lot of activity at the boundary between the liquid and the vapour.

The text explains that thus, at equilibrium, the rate of evaporation is equal to the rate of condensation.

The text explains that it may be represented by H 2 O (l) H2 O (vap) The double half arrows indicate that the processes in both the directions are going on simultaneously.

The text explains that the mixture of reactants and products in the equilibrium state is called an equilibrium mixture.

The text explains that equilibrium can be established for both physical processes and chemical reactions.

The text explains that the reaction may be fast or slow depending on the experimental conditions and the nature of the reactants.

The text explains that when the reactants in a closed vessel at a particular temperature react to give products, the concentrations of the reactants keep on decreasing, while those of products keep on increasing for some time after which there is no change in the concentrations of either of the reactants or products.

The text explains that this stage of the system is the dynamic equilibrium and the rates of the forward and reverse reactions become equal.

The text explains that it is due to After studying this unit you will be able to • identify dynamic nature of equilibrium involved in physical and chemical processes; • state the law of equilibrium; • e x p l a i n c h a r a c t e r i s t i c s o f equilibria involved in physical and chemical processes; • write expressions for equilibrium constants; • establish a relationship between Kp and K c ; • explain various factors that affect the equilibrium state of a reaction; • classify substances as acids or bases according to Arrhenius, Bronsted-Lowry and Lewis concepts; • classify acids and bases as weak or strong in terms of their ionization constants; • explain the dependence of degree of ionization on concentration of the electrolyte and that of the common ion; • describe pH scale for representing hydrogen ion concentration; • explain ionisation of water and its duel role as acid and base; • describe ionic product (Kw ) and pKw for water; • a p p r e c i a t e u s e o f b u f f e r solutions; • calculate solubility product constant.

The text explains that 169 EQUILIBRIUM this dynamic equilibrium stage that there is no change in the concentrations of various species in the reaction mixture.

The text explains that (iii) The reactions in which the concentrations of the reactants and products are comparable, when the system is in equilibrium.

The text explains that the extent of a reaction in equilibrium varies with the experimental conditions such as concentrations of reactants, temperature, etc.

The text explains that optimisation of the operational conditions is very important in industry and laboratory so that equilibrium is favorable in the direction of the desired product.

The text explains that some important aspects of equilibrium involving physical and chemical processes are dealt in this unit along with the equilibrium involving ions in aqueous solutions which is called as ionic equilibrium.

The text explains that 6.1 E q U i l i b r i U m i n P H Y S i C A l PrOCESSES The characteristics of system at equilibrium are better understood if we examine some physical processes.

The text explains that the most familiar examples are phase transformation processes, e.g., solid liquid liquid gas solid gas 6.1.1 Solid-liquid Equilibrium Ice and water kept in a perfectly insulated thermos flask (no exchange of heat between its contents and the surroundings) at 273K and the atmospheric pressure are in equilibrium state and the system shows interesting characteristic features.

The text explains that we observe that the mass of ice and water do not change with time and the temperature remains constant.

The text explains that there is no change of mass of ice and water, as the rates of transfer of molecules from ice into water and of reverse transfer from water into ice are equal at atmospheric pressure and 273 K.

The text explains that it is obvious that ice and water are in equilibrium only at particular temperature and pressure.

The text explains that for any pure substance at atmospheric pressure, the temperature at which the solid and liquid phases are at equilibrium is called the normal melting point or normal freezing point of the substance.

The text explains that the system here is in dynamic equilibrium and we can infer the following: (i) Both the opposing processes occur simultaneously.

The text explains that (ii) Both the processes occur at the same rate so that the amount of ice and water remains constant.

The text explains that it will be observed that the mercury level in the right limb of the manometer slowly increases and finally attains a constant value, that is, the pressure inside the box increases and reaches a constant value.

The text explains that as water evaporated the pressure in the box increased due to addition of water molecules into the gaseous phase inside the box.

The text explains that 170 CHEMISTRY However, the rate of increase in pressure decreases with time due to condensation of vapour into water.

The text explains that this implies that the number of water molecules from the gaseous state into the liquid state also increases till the equilibrium is attained i.e., rate of evaporation= rate of condensation H2 O(l) H2 O (vap) At equilibrium the pressure exerted by the water molecules at a given temperature remains constant and is called the equilibrium vapour pressure of water (or just vapour pressure of water); vapour pressure of water increases with temperature.

The text explains that if the above experiment is repeated with methyl alcohol, acetone and ether, it is observed that different liquids have different equilibrium vapour pressures at the same temperature, and the liquid which has a higher vapour pressure is more volatile and has a lower boiling point.

The text explains that if we expose three watch glasses containing separately 1mL each of acetone, ethyl alcohol, and water to atmosphere and repeat the experiment with different volumes of the liquids in a warmer room, it is observed that in all such cases the liquid eventually disappears and the time taken for complete evaporation depends on (i) the nature of the liquid, (ii) the amount of the liquid and (iii) the temperature.

The text explains that these are open systems and it is not possible to reach equilibrium in an open system.

The text explains that water and water vapour are in equilibrium position at atmospheric pressure (1.013 bar) and at 100°C in a closed vessel.

The text explains that the boiling point of water is 100°C at 1.013 bar pressure.

The text explains that for any pure liquid at one atmospheric pressure (1.013 bar), the temperature at which the liquid and vapours are at equilibrium is called normal boiling point of the liquid.

The text explains that boiling point of the liquid depends on the atmospheric pressure.

The text explains that 6.1.3 Solid – Vapour Equilibrium Let us now consider the systems where solids sublime to vapour phase.

The text explains that 6.1 Measuring equilibrium vapour pressure of water at a constant temperature

The text explains that 171 EQUILIBRIUM 6.1.4 Equilibrium Involving Dissolution of Solid or Gases in Liquids Solids in liquids We know from our experience that we can dissolve only a limited amount of salt or sugar in a given amount of water at room temperature.

The text explains that if we make a thick sugar syrup solution by dissolving sugar at a higher temperature, sugar crystals separate out if we cool the syrup to the room temperature.

The text explains that we call it a saturated solution when no more of solute can be dissolved in it at a given temperature.

The text explains that will it lead to precipitation of copper iodate?

The text explains that 6.70 The ionization constant of benzoic acid is 6.46 × 10 –5 and Ksp for silver benzoate is 2.5 × 10 –13 .

The text explains that how many times is silver benzoate more soluble in a buffer of pH 3.19 compared to its solubility in pure water?

The text explains that 6.71 What is the maximum concentration of equimolar solutions of ferrous sulphate and sodium sulphide so that when mixed in equal volumes, there is no precipitation of iron sulphide?

The text explains that 6.72 What is the minimum volume of water required to dissolve 1g of calcium sulphate at 298 K?

The text explains that 6.73 The concentration of sulphide ion in 0.1M HCl solution saturated with hydrogen sulphide is 1.0 × 10 –19 M.

The text explains that if 10 mL of this is added to 5 mL of 0.04 M solution of the following: FeSO4 , MnCl2 , ZnCl2 and CdCl2 . in which of these solutions precipitation will take place?

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Thermodynamics, which statement is supported by the source text about 170 CHEMISTRY?
options: Based on the extent to which the reactions proceed to reach the state of chemical equilibrium, these may be classified in three groups.
(i) The reactions that proceed nearly to completion and only negligible concentrations of the reactants are left.
In some cases, it may not be even possible to detect these experimentally.
(ii) The reactions in which only small amounts of products are formed and most of the reactants remain unchanged at equilibrium stage.
